mission.title: Install and Configure Emissary Ingress on Kubernetes
mission.description: Emissary-ingress is an open-source, Kubernetes-native API gateway built on the Envoy Proxy, designed to simplify the ingress problem for cloud-native applications. Installing Emissary-ingress allows developers to manage ingress traffic without needing deep Kubernetes expertise.

steps.title: Add the Emissary-ingress Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Emissary-ingress Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add emissary-ingress https://getambassador.io/helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Emissary-ingress using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Emissary-ingress in a new namespace called 'emissary' with the following command:
```bash
helm install emissary-ingress emissary-ingress/emissary --namespace emissary --create-namespace --version 3.10.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Emissary-ingress pods are running successfully by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace emissary
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure your Emissary-ingress deployment has appropriate resource limits, edit the deployment with:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment emissary-ingress --namespace emissary
```
Add the following under `spec.template.spec.containers.resources`:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the Emissary-ingress service, set up port forwarding with:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/emissary-ingress --namespace emissary 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Emissary-ingress will show the pods running when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace emissary`. You should also be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add emissary-ingress https://getambassador.io/helm
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Emissary-ingress release, execute the following command to uninstall it from the 'emissary' namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall emissary-ingress --namespace emissary
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources associated with Emissary-ingress by running:
```bash
kubectl delete crd -l app=emissary-ingress
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'emissary' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace emissary
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of the Emissary-ingress release. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values emissary-ingress --namespace emissary > emissary-ingress-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Emissary-ingress release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade emissary-ingress emissary-ingress/emissary --namespace emissary --version 3.11.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Emissary-ingress pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace emissary
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that the Emissary-ingress pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace emissary
```
Common issues may include misconfigurations in the deployment or insufficient resources.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the Emissary-ingress service is not reachable, ensure that the service is running and check the port forwarding:
```bash
kubectl get services --namespace emissary
kubectl port-forward service/emissary-ingress --namespace emissary 8080:80
```

troubleshooting.title: Ingress rules not working
troubleshooting.description:
If your ingress rules are not functioning as expected, verify the configuration of your ingress resources:
```bash
kubectl get ingress --namespace emissary
kubectl describe ingress <ingress-name> --namespace emissary
```

troubleshooting.title: High memory usage
troubleshooting.description:
If you observe high memory usage from the Emissary-ingress pods, consider adjusting the resource limits. Check the current resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace emissary
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
